cards.headline: The war everyone hoped was over came back overnight: after Iranian forces hit three tankers in the Strait of Hormuz, the US struck more than 80 targets across Iran and 60-plus Revolutionary Guard boats, Iran fired back at American bases in Bahrain and Kuwait, and Trump declared the ceasefire 'over' and said the US will 'probably hit them hard again tonight' — sending oil toward $80, halting tanker traffic through the world's key oil gate, crashing Indian stocks 1,677 points and sinking the rupee, all as Iran buries Khamenei in Mashhad with his unseen successor barred from his own father's funeral and TCS opens India's earnings season into the storm.
cards.big_picture: The single fact that reorganizes everything today is that the US-Iran truce didn't just crack, it broke. After Iranian forces struck three tankers in the Strait of Hormuz, US forces hit more than 80 targets across Iran and over 60 Revolutionary Guard boats; Iran fired drones and missiles back at American bases in Bahrain and Kuwait; and Trump, at the NATO summit in Ankara, said the ceasefire is 'over' and that Washington will 'probably hit them hard again tonight.' That is why oil leapt toward $80 a barrel and ships essentially stopped moving through the chokepoint that carries a fifth of the world's oil. The shock lands hardest exactly where we've warned for a week it would — on oil-importing India, which gets roughly 40% of its crude, 60% of its gas and 90% of its cooking gas through Hormuz: the Sensex fell 1,677 points for its worst day in months, the rupee sank to 95.55 per dollar, and IT giant TCS now opens earnings season tonight into a frightened market. The rest of the board is the same reality-check in different clothes: Iran buries Khamenei in Mashhad while the son who replaced him is barred from attending for fear of assassination; the Federal Reserve's first minutes under new chair Kevin Warsh reveal a committee more worried about inflation than about cutting rates, which firmed the dollar and — counter-intuitively on a war day — pushed gold lower; and the only real green shoot is narrow, as China easing the way for its firms to buy Nvidia's H200 chips lifted Nvidia against an otherwise red tape. The throughline: a world that spent June convincing itself the war was finished is being forced to price the possibility that it has simply resumed.
cards.market_headline: War risk goes live: the US and Iran trade strikes, oil rockets toward $80 and Hormuz shipping halts — Indian stocks suffer their worst day in months, the rupee hits a record low, and TCS opens earnings season tonight into the storm.

cards.title: The US strikes Iran and calls the ceasefire 'over' — oil spikes toward $80 as Hormuz shuts
cards.what_happened: After Iranian forces attacked three commercial vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz, US Central Command launched what it called 'powerful' strikes on more than 80 targets across Iran — air-defence systems, command-and-control networks, coastal radar and anti-ship missile sites — plus over 60 Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps small boats in and around the strait. Iran's Guard retaliated with drone and missile strikes on US military facilities in Bahrain and Kuwait, several of which Kuwaiti forces said they intercepted. Speaking at the NATO summit in Ankara, Trump said 'we hit them very hard last night, and we'll probably hit them hard again tonight,' declared the ceasefire 'over,' floated seizing Iran's main oil-export terminal at Kharg Island, and claimed Iran is plotting to assassinate him — while also saying he did not think the wider conflict would 'start again.' The US Treasury revoked the licence that had let Iran sell crude. Brent crude jumped about 5% toward $80 a barrel and tanker traffic through Hormuz 'essentially stopped.'
key_points: US hit 80+ Iranian targets and 60+ Revolutionary Guard boats
Iran fired drones and missiles back at US bases in Bahrain and Kuwait
Trump: ceasefire 'over,' 'we'll probably hit them hard again tonight'
Brent spiked ~5% toward $80; tanker traffic through Hormuz 'essentially stopped'
cards.moot: A chokepoint is leverage: when a fifth of the world's oil must squeeze through one strait, an exchange of strikes there can move global prices more than any change in how much is pumped — and a ceasefire is a pause that can be un-paused, so markets must keep pricing the risk it holds.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported that projectiles struck a Qatari LNG tanker and a Saudi supertanker in Hormuz — the biggest attack since June's peace deal — breaking the US-Iran detente and jolting WTI up ~3.3% to ~$72.75, and warned the risk premium could 'build from here.'
thread.changed: The confrontation has gone from tanker strikes to open warfare: a third ship was hit, the US struck 80+ targets in Iran and 60+ Guard boats, Iran fired back at US bases in Bahrain and Kuwait, Trump declared the ceasefire 'over' and revoked Iran's oil licence, and Brent spiked toward $80 as Hormuz traffic halted.

depth.background: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow sea gate between Iran and Oman through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil and much of its liquefied natural gas must pass — there is no easy detour. After a three-month US-Israel war with Iran earlier this year, the two sides reached a late-June understanding to reopen the strait and stop attacks on shipping. For a week we flagged that this calm 'rests on Iran keeping the Hormuz ceasefire,' and that any fresh incident 'would put a risk premium straight back into oil.' Yesterday two tankers were struck; today that risk detonated in full.
depth.why_it_matters: This is the difference between a scare and a shooting war reopening. Even short of a full closure, live strikes and halted tanker traffic drive up insurance, reroute shipping and inject a large 'war premium' into energy — and because oil and gas set the price of almost everything, a burning Hormuz is a tax on the entire global economy. It also drags in the Gulf states hosting US bases and raises the odds of a wider regional conflict, with each side now trading blows rather than threats.
depth.ripple_effects: Oil, gas and shipping costs climb; energy exporters gain while importers lose. India, which buys about 85% of its oil abroad and routes most Gulf energy through Hormuz, is hit first — a sustained spike widens its import bill, weakens the rupee, revives inflation and erodes the cheap-oil tailwind that had powered its market. Airlines, paint, tyre and logistics firms that burn fuel get squeezed; defence and oil producers benefit. A firmer dollar and 'risk-off' mood pull money out of emerging markets and speculative assets worldwide.
depth.why_now: Iran's attack on three tankers crossed a red line the US said it would answer, and Washington chose to answer with force rather than diplomacy — turning a fragile pause into an active exchange of strikes just as Iran's leadership is consumed by Khamenei's funeral.
depth.watch_next: Whether the US strikes again tonight as Trump threatened, whether Iran moves to close or mine Hormuz, and how far oil climbs (Brent ~$80, WTI ~$74).
depth.market_link: Directly lifts crude (Brent ~$80, WTI ~$74) and gas, pressures oil-importer currencies like the rupee, sinks equities in import-heavy markets like India, and supports energy producers and defence names; a firmer dollar keeps 'risk-off' pressure on crypto and growth stocks.

key_terms.definition: A narrow sea lane between Iran and Oman carrying roughly a fifth of the world's seaborne oil and much of its gas; there is no easy way around it.
The extra amount buyers pay for oil to insure against conflict cutting off supply, even before a single barrel is actually lost.
Iran's elite military force, which controls fast-attack boats and missiles used to threaten Gulf shipping.

cards.title: India's worst market day in months — Sensex sheds 1,677 points, rupee hits 95.55 as the oil gate to India closes
cards.what_happened: The Sensex tanked 1,677.12 points (2.15%) to close at 76,503.60 — during the day it had plunged as much as 1,921 points to 76,259 — while the Nifty 50 fell 516.65 points (2.12%) to 23,882.05, the worst session in over three months, as renewed US-Iran fighting and the oil spike drained risk appetite. The rupee tumbled 59 paise to a record-low close of 95.55 per dollar, hurt by both costlier oil (more dollars needed to pay for imports) and a stronger dollar globally. The damage was concrete: state refiner MRPL cancelled a West Asia crude-cargo charter and scrambled for a replacement vessel, and India's foreign ministry urged 'restraint' and a 'return to diplomacy.'
key_points: Worst session in over three months; intraday plunge topped 1,900 points
Rupee sank 59 paise to a fresh low of 95.55 per dollar
Hormuz carries ~40% of India's crude, ~60% of gas, ~90% of cooking gas
cards.moot: The force that drives a rally usually drives the reversal too — an oil-importing market that rose on cheap crude is the same market that falls fastest when a single strait is threatened, because geography, not just the balance sheet, sets the price.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported India's benchmarks snapping a five-day rally and gapping down as the first Hormuz tanker attacks spiked oil, with the Nifty near 24,286 and the rupee around 95.2, ahead of TCS earnings.
thread.changed: The wobble has become a rout: full-blown US-Iran fighting sent oil toward $80, the Sensex crashed 1,677 points to 76,504 (its worst day in months), the Nifty fell to 23,882 and the rupee hit a record-low 95.55.

depth.background: India's benchmarks — the Nifty 50 and the 30-stock Sensex — had rallied for most of the past fortnight on cheap oil, because India imports about 85% of its crude and a lower oil price eases inflation, the import bill and pressure on the rupee all at once. That entire tailwind hinged on a calm Strait of Hormuz. When the US-Iran ceasefire broke overnight and oil spiked toward $80, the trade that lifted India for a week reversed in a single session.
depth.why_it_matters: This is the mirror image of the rally, and it exposes how tightly India is chained to the oil price. Hormuz alone handles roughly 40% of India's crude imports, about 60% of its liquefied natural gas and around 90% of its LPG (cooking gas) — so a threat to that one waterway touches fuel, factories, transport and kitchens at once. A weaker rupee makes every imported barrel dearer, feeding inflation and giving the Reserve Bank of India less room to cut interest rates.
depth.ripple_effects: Costlier fuel squeezes oil-consuming sectors — airlines, paints, tyres, logistics, cement — and pressures household budgets; a sinking rupee raises the cost of imports and foreign debt. Foreign investors, already cautious, tend to sell emerging markets in a risk-off, strong-dollar moment. If oil stays high, expect wider trade and current-account deficits and a more hawkish RBI. A quick de-escalation would reverse much of this, which is why every Hormuz headline now moves Dalal Street.
depth.why_now: The overnight US strikes on Iran and Trump's 'ceasefire is over' broke the cheap-oil trade at the open, and India — as one of the world's most oil-import-dependent large economies — was always going to feel a Hormuz shock first and hardest.
depth.watch_next: The crude price and whether the rupee breaches 96, foreign-investor (FII) selling flows, and TCS's after-close results tonight as the first earnings test into the shock.
depth.market_link: Directly moves the Nifty/Sensex, the rupee, oil-consuming stocks and India's rate outlook; TCS's tonight print now lands into a shaken market.

key_terms.definition: The total cost of everything a country buys from abroad; for India, oil is a huge chunk, so a higher oil price swells it and weakens the currency.
Liquefied petroleum gas — the bottled cooking gas used in most Indian kitchens; India imports most of it, much of it via Hormuz.
A market mood where investors flee riskier assets (like emerging-market stocks) for safer ones (like the dollar), usually during shocks or conflict.

cards.title: Iran buries Khamenei in Mashhad — and bars its own new Supreme Leader from the funeral
cards.what_happened: Khamenei's body is being buried today at the revered Imam Reza shrine in Mashhad, Iran's holiest city, after processions through Qom and Iraq's Karbala. In a striking sign of the danger the leadership feels, Mojtaba was barred from attending his own father's funeral over fears Israel would try to assassinate him; instead his three other sons — Mostafa, Meysam and Masoud — attended. He has still made no public appearance as leader. The burial unfolds against the day's dramatic backdrop of the US and Iran exchanging strikes.
key_points: Burial today, 9 July, at the Imam Reza shrine in Mashhad
Successor Mojtaba barred from attending over assassination fears
His three sons attended in his place; he still hasn't appeared in public
The rites unfold as Iran and the US trade fresh strikes
cards.moot: In systems built around one person, hiding the leader is itself information — it tells you how threatened a state feels and how uncertain its chain of command is, and rivals and markets probe that uncertainty the moment it shows.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported the Mashhad burial set for Wednesday, with successor Mojtaba still unseen and reported wounded in the February strike, and the succession question turning consequential as tankers were hit in Hormuz.
thread.changed: The burial is now under way, and the leadership has openly barred Mojtaba from attending his father's funeral for fear of assassination — sending his three other sons instead — as Iran and the US exchange fresh strikes.

depth.background: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei ruled Iran for over three decades and was killed in a US-Israeli airstrike on 28 February. His son Mojtaba was chosen as Supreme Leader — Iran's highest authority, outranking the elected president — by the Assembly of Experts in March, but has not been seen in public since, communicating only in writing; US and other reports say he was wounded and possibly disfigured in the strike that killed his father. A week-long, multi-city state funeral began on 3 July and reached its emotional peak this week.
depth.why_it_matters: A nation of 90 million sitting on some of the world's largest oil and gas reserves — and controlling Hormuz — is being led by a man no one has seen, at the exact moment it is trading blows with the United States. In a system built around one supreme figure, keeping that figure hidden signals both fear of assassination and deep uncertainty about who is actually making Iran's wartime decisions.
depth.ripple_effects: The succession fog plus live conflict is precisely what oil markets dread: it keeps a fat risk premium in crude and gas, which flows straight to import-dependent economies like India. If hardliners are steering policy in Mojtaba's absence, more strikes and Hormuz disruption become likelier; a visible, pragmatic leader would calm markets. Either way, Iranian political risk is now being priced in real time.
depth.why_now: The week-long funeral rites culminate in today's burial, and the leadership's decision to hide its new Supreme Leader has become sharper and more consequential now that the country is again exchanging fire with the US.
depth.watch_next: Whether Mojtaba appears or speaks at all after the burial, and whether Iran's next move on Hormuz signals hardline or pragmatic control.
depth.market_link: Iran's leadership uncertainty amid live strikes keeps the oil risk premium elevated (Brent ~$80) — a direct concern for oil-importer India and a tailwind for energy producers.

key_terms.definition: Iran's highest authority — a cleric who outranks the elected president and controls the military, judiciary and state media.
A vast, revered Shia religious complex in Mashhad, Iran's holiest city and the chosen burial site.

cards.title: TCS opens India's earnings season tonight — into a crashing, oil-shocked market
cards.what_happened: TCS will report its Q1 FY27 (April-June) results after the close today, followed by a 7pm IST earnings call. Analysts expect revenue near Rs 72,012 crore (about 1.9% higher than last quarter), with dollar revenue around $7.6 billion — essentially flat quarter-on-quarter — and net profit slipping roughly 2.5% from the previous quarter as annual wage hikes eat into margins. The board is also expected to declare an interim dividend (it paid Rs 11 per share a year ago), with a record date of 15 July. The print lands on a day the market just suffered its worst session in months.
key_points: TCS reports Q1 FY27 after market close today; call at 7pm IST
Revenue seen ~Rs 72,012 cr (+1.9% QoQ); dollar revenue ~$7.6bn, roughly flat
Profit seen dipping ~2.5% QoQ as annual wage hikes squeeze margins
An interim dividend is expected; record date 15 July
cards.moot: Markets move ahead of the news, then judge it against expectations — a result that merely matches a modest forecast can still disappoint if hopes had run higher, so the commentary and guidance often matter more than the headline number.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported TCS due to open Q1 FY27 season on 9 July with a dividend on the table, as the market gapped down on the Hormuz oil spike.
thread.changed: That day is here: TCS reports tonight (call at 7pm IST) with revenue seen ~Rs 72,012 cr and profit dipping on wage hikes — now landing into the market's worst session in months after the US-Iran escalation.

depth.background: Tata Consultancy Services (TCS) is India's largest IT-services firm — it builds and runs software for global clients and earns most of its money in dollars. It traditionally opens India's quarterly earnings season, so its numbers and, more importantly, its commentary set the tone for the whole IT sector and much of the market. This year the sector has been under a cloud over whether artificial intelligence will erode demand for outsourced coding, making today's read especially watched.
depth.why_it_matters: Earnings season is the reality check that decides whether prices rest on real profits or on hope. As the first big result, TCS is the market's proxy for the entire IT sector and a heavyweight in the indices, so its numbers swing sentiment far beyond one stock. And with the market rattled by the oil shock, a reassuring TCS could steady nerves while a weak one would deepen the gloom — and its verdict on AI (threat or new demand?) matters most of all.
depth.ripple_effects: Strong revenue and upbeat AI-era order commentary would lift Infosys, HCLTech, Wipro and the broader Nifty and could tempt foreign buyers back; a miss or cautious guidance would hit IT hardest and puncture confidence just as the Iran shock bites. A weaker rupee is a quiet tailwind for these dollar-earners, softening the margin hit from wage hikes.
depth.why_now: TCS has fixed today for its results, so an oil shock and the season's most important earnings test have collapsed into the same 24 hours.
depth.watch_next: TCS's actual revenue, margin and dividend versus the estimates above — and its management's commentary on AI and client-spending demand, on tonight's 7pm call.
depth.market_link: Directly drives TCS, Infosys and the Nifty/Sensex; the print and AI commentary set the tone for India's whole Q1 season, and a soft rupee cushions the exporters' margins.

key_terms.definition: India's first fiscal quarter — April to June 2026; Indian firms report on an April-March financial year.
A cash payout to shareholders declared partway through the financial year, before the final full-year dividend.
The share of revenue a company keeps as profit; wage hikes lower it by raising costs faster than sales.

cards.title: NATO summit wraps: €70bn for Ukraine, and Trump lets Kyiv build its own Patriot missiles
cards.what_happened: As the summit closed, allies confirmed roughly €70bn ($80bn) of military aid to Ukraine for 2026, and Trump said in a notably friendly appearance with President Zelensky that the US will give Ukraine a licence to produce Patriot missile interceptors itself — a shift from merely supplying them. A group of European allies, convened in part by UK PM Keir Starmer, separately announced about £37bn for a new missile programme. Trump praised 'progress' toward peace even as he simultaneously reopened the fight with Iran.
key_points: Ankara summit confirms ~€70bn ($80bn) in 2026 aid for Ukraine
Trump says the US will license Ukraine to produce Patriot interceptors
Allies also unveil a ~£37bn European missile project
But Patriots take years to build — relief is not immediate
cards.moot: Owning the factory beats owning the stockpile — a licence to build your own weapons changes a country's leverage far more than any single shipment, but manufacturing capacity takes years to stand up, so the relief lags the announcement.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported the Ankara summit confirming a ~€70bn Ukraine pledge that was mostly repackaged funds with no US contribution, with Zelensky dropped from the main session and meeting Trump only on the sidelines to beg for Patriots.
thread.changed: The summit closed with a bigger deliverable than expected — Trump agreeing to license Ukraine to produce Patriot interceptors itself, plus a ~£37bn European missile project — though the interceptors' long build time means the near-term shortage persists.

depth.background: NATO is the 32-nation military alliance binding the US, Canada and most of Europe under a promise of mutual defence. Its Ankara summit (7-8 July) was meant to enforce last year's pledge to raise members' defence spending toward 5% of GDP and to keep backing Ukraine — made urgent by Russia's recent record barrage, which exposed that Kyiv's US-made Patriot air-defence systems had nearly run out of interceptor missiles. The Patriot is the main weapon that can reliably shoot down Russia's fastest ballistic missiles.
depth.why_it_matters: Letting Ukraine build Patriots is potentially a bigger deal than any one aid cheque — it moves Kyiv toward making its own air defences rather than depending on scarce US stockpiles. But the catch is time: Patriot interceptors are complex and slow to manufacture, so a production licence eases the long-run supply squeeze, not tonight's shortage. And Europe pledging tens of billions for its own missiles signals a continent rearming for a world where it can no longer assume unlimited US cover.
depth.ripple_effects: Defence contractors in the US and Europe gain from new orders and the production deal; Ukraine's longer-term air defence steadies, though the near-term gap remains. Hundreds of billions redirected to defence reshapes European budgets. An under-defended Ukraine in the meantime keeps a latent war premium in energy — which now stacks on top of today's much larger Iran-driven oil spike.
depth.why_now: The summit was scheduled for 7-8 July; the interceptor shortage turned a routine budget meeting into an emergency about whether Kyiv can keep defending its skies, and the Patriot-production decision was the headline deliverable as it wrapped.
depth.watch_next: How fast the Patriot-production licence translates into actual interceptors, and whether the €70bn is fresh money or repackaged commitments.
depth.market_link: Supportive for US and European defence contractors; a still-under-defended Ukraine keeps a war premium latent in European gas, compounding today's Iran-driven crude spike.

key_terms.definition: The missile a US-made Patriot battery fires to destroy an incoming ballistic missile; costly and slow to build, so stockpiles can run dry.
Legal permission to manufacture a weapon domestically rather than only buy it from the original maker.
A defence-spending target measured against the size of a country's economy; moving toward it is an enormous jump in military budgets.

cards.title: The Fed's first Warsh-era minutes reveal a committee split — and more worried about inflation than cuts
cards.what_happened: The minutes, released 8 July, showed the Fed voted unanimously (12-0) to hold its benchmark rate at 3.5%-3.75%, but that officials were sharply split on what comes next: some saw inflation easing enough to allow lower rates, while many others thought the appropriate rate would end 2026 above the current range — meaning a hike is on the table. Concern that price increases were 'broadening' was described as mounting. Warsh released a stripped-down, 130-word statement with no forward guidance and declined to submit his own rate projection, calling the debate a 'family fight' and stressing the Fed should react to data rather than pre-commit to a path.
key_points: June minutes: unanimous 12-0 hold at 3.5%-3.75%
Officials split; many saw rates ending 2026 above today's level
Inflation worries 'mounting' and 'broadening'
Warsh dropped forward guidance and skipped his own rate forecast
cards.moot: When leadership changes at a central bank, markets must re-learn its reaction function from scratch — so early documents move prices more than usual, and a bank that fights inflation over growth tends to lift the dollar and weigh on everything priced against it.

thread.previously: For several days we flagged Wednesday's first Warsh-era Fed minutes as 'the next real signal,' noting a Fed 'stuck' by weak June hiring (57,000 jobs) and sticky inflation, with Warsh signalling no cuts this year.
thread.changed: The minutes are out: the Fed held 12-0 but revealed a split committee with inflation worries mounting and many members expecting rates to end 2026 higher — a hawkish tilt that firmed the dollar and helped push gold down.

depth.background: The Federal Reserve sets US interest rates to balance jobs against inflation; because the dollar anchors the world, its stance ripples everywhere. It is now led by new chair Kevin Warsh, whose first meeting in June was closely watched for how he'd run policy. 'Minutes' are the detailed written record of a Fed meeting, released weeks later, that traders mine for clues the short statement didn't give. This was the first minutes of the Warsh era.
depth.why_it_matters: For a market hoping for rate cuts, this leans hawkish — 'hawkish' meaning tilted toward keeping rates high or raising them to fight inflation. A Fed that won't cut, and might even hike, keeps 'risk-free' bond yields attractive, which strengthens the dollar and pressures everything that competes with cash: growth stocks, gold and emerging-market currencies. With a new chair and no track record, every early signal like this moves prices more than usual.
depth.ripple_effects: A firmer dollar keeps the rupee soft (95.55) and makes foreign investors cautious toward India. It is a big reason gold fell even on a war day — higher-for-longer rates raise the opportunity cost of holding metal that pays no interest. Rate-sensitive tech and crypto stay under pressure; borrowers face no relief. Markets now lean on the 29 July rate decision for the next real signal.
depth.why_now: The June minutes were released on schedule on 8 July — the first primary window into how the new Warsh-led Fed reasons, landing the same day the Iran shock revived inflation fears via oil.
depth.watch_next: The 29 July Fed rate decision, and whether the oil spike pushes the next US inflation print high enough to turn 'no cuts' into 'possible hike.'
depth.market_link: A hawkish Fed firms the dollar, pressures gold, keeps the rupee soft and foreign flows cautious toward India, and weighs on rate-sensitive tech and crypto worldwide.

key_terms.definition: The detailed written record of a Federal Reserve meeting, released weeks later; traders read it for clues about future rate moves.
A stance that favours higher interest rates to fight inflation, even at the cost of slower growth — the opposite of 'dovish.'
When a central bank tells markets in advance where it expects to steer rates; Warsh has dropped it in favour of reacting to data.

cards.title: China cracks open the door to Nvidia's H200 chips — and Nvidia jumps against a red market
cards.what_happened: According to reporting from The Information, Chinese officials have told leading AI companies — Alibaba, ByteDance and DeepSeek among them — that they may soon receive permission to buy a limited quantity of Nvidia H200 chips, provided they justify how many they need and why. It marks a softening of China's earlier reluctance and would help offset a shortage caused by surging AI demand. The news lifted Nvidia about 3% to roughly $203, one of the few gainers on a day the broader US market fell on the Iran escalation.
key_points: China to let top AI firms buy a limited amount of Nvidia H200 chips
Alibaba, ByteDance and DeepSeek told they may soon get approval
A softening of strict export limits on advanced US AI chips
Nvidia rose ~3% even as the Dow fell ~1% on the Iran shock
cards.moot: In a market led by one theme, news that widens that theme's addressable market can lift a single stock even against a falling tape — but policy-driven gains are only as durable as the policy, which can flip with a headline.

depth.background: Nvidia makes the graphics chips that train and run most modern AI, and its most advanced models have been at the centre of a US-China tech fight: Washington restricts exports of top chips to China on national-security grounds, and Beijing has at times discouraged its firms from buying them. The H200 is a powerful (though not top-tier) Nvidia AI chip. Any thaw in who can buy what is a big deal for the company and for the global AI build-out.
depth.why_it_matters: The AI-chip trade has been the load-bearing wall of this market, so anything that widens Nvidia's biggest potential market — China — is a genuine positive for the whole AI complex. It also shows the US-China tech relationship bending toward pragmatic commerce even amid rivalry: China wants the compute, and Nvidia wants the sales. On a fearful, oil-shocked day, it was the rare piece of good news for risk assets.
depth.ripple_effects: A friendlier China channel supports Nvidia, its suppliers and AI-infrastructure names, and by extension sentiment toward Indian IT, which rides the same AI-demand narrative. But 'limited' and 'case-by-case' are the key words — the approvals are capped and conditional, and export policy can reverse quickly, so this is a crack in the door, not an open one.
depth.why_now: The report surfaced on 8 July amid a global AI-chip shortage that gives both sides an incentive to ease restrictions — Chinese firms need the hardware, Nvidia wants the revenue.
depth.watch_next: Whether the approvals are formalised and how large the quotas are, plus any US response to easier H200 sales into China.
depth.market_link: Directly lifts Nvidia (+~3% to ~$203) and the AI-chip complex; a positive read-through for AI-infrastructure names and Indian IT sentiment, though capped by 'limited, case-by-case' approvals.

key_terms.definition: Government limits on selling sensitive technology (like advanced chips) to certain countries, usually on national-security grounds.
A high-end Nvidia AI accelerator chip — powerful but a step below its very top models — sought after for training AI systems.

cards.title: India's monsoon is 15% short for the season — yet 'bursts' of rain are drowning cities and hills
cards.what_happened: India is running about 15.2% below normal rainfall for the season so far, yet several states are being battered by sudden downpours. In Kerala's Wayanad, a debris slip killed three people with five still missing and about 129 shifted to relief camps; Delhi and Mumbai saw rain havoc, including a three-storey under-construction building collapsing in Delhi's Rohini and a garbage mound burying people in Maharashtra's Pimpri Chinchwad; and floods and landslides hit multiple Arunachal Pradesh districts. Across the border, a monsoon-triggered landslide killed eight at a girls' school in Bangladesh.
key_points: Season rainfall runs about 15.2% below normal overall
Yet violent bursts flood Delhi, Mumbai, Kerala and the Northeast
Wayanad debris slip kills 3; ~129 moved to relief camps
A Delhi building collapse and Arunachal landslides add to the toll
cards.moot: In a farm-heavy economy the weather is a macro variable, and a warming climate makes it a double threat — the same season can be dry on average yet deadly in bursts, hitting crops and cities at once and tying a central bank's hands.

thread.previously: Yesterday we explained the below-normal monsoon (~92% of the long-period average) as a slow-burning risk to food inflation and the RBI's rate path.
thread.changed: The picture has turned acute and paradoxical: the season is ~15.2% short overall, yet intense bursts are killing people and flooding Kerala, Delhi, Mumbai and the Northeast — the same year proving both too dry and too wet.

depth.background: The southwest monsoon (June-September) is India's real annual economic event: it waters more than half the country's farmland and decides food prices, rural incomes and how much room the Reserve Bank of India has to cut rates. A 'below-normal' season sounds like a drought risk — but climate change is making the rain arrive in short, intense bursts rather than steady spells, so the same year can be dry on average yet deadly in bursts.
depth.why_it_matters: This is the worst of both worlds for a farm-heavy economy. A below-normal total threatens crops, food prices and rural demand — the biggest chunk of India's inflation basket — while violent bursts cause floods, deaths and infrastructure damage in the same season. Layered on top of today's oil spike, it removes two of the tailwinds (cheap food, cheap fuel) that had kept India's inflation tame, and keeps the RBI cautious about cutting rates.
depth.ripple_effects: Patchy, badly-distributed rain lifts food inflation and squeezes rural spending on two-wheelers, tractors and consumer goods; floods raise disaster-relief costs and disrupt cities. A strong recovery in the rains through July-August would ease the picture; a persistent deficit with continued flooding would pressure both growth and prices, and keep policymakers on hold.
depth.why_now: The monsoon is mid-season, the rainfall deficit has widened even as intense bursts cause flooding, and it collides with a fresh oil shock — turning a background concern into a live one for inflation and rates.
depth.watch_next: July-August rainfall distribution (not just the total), the next CPI inflation print, and the RBI's tone at its coming policy meeting.
depth.market_link: Shapes India's food inflation, the RBI's rate path, and rural-demand and consumer stocks; combined with the oil spike, it pressures the rupee and bond yields.

key_terms.definition: The typical monsoon rainfall over decades; the season is judged 'normal,' 'above' or 'below' relative to this benchmark.
How rain is spread across regions and weeks — a good total can still mean disaster if it falls in a few violent bursts and leaves gaps elsewhere.
The Consumer Price Index measures how fast the prices households pay are rising; food is its single biggest component in India.

cards.title: US launches a sweeping H-1B visa-fraud probe — Indian IT firms and workers in the spotlight
cards.what_happened: The US Department of Labor's Inspector General, Anthony D'Esposito, announced what officials called one of the most aggressive investigations yet into employment-based visa fraud — covering the H-1B and PERM systems and alleging fraudulent applications, coercive wage-kickback arrangements, forced-labour practices and the displacement of American workers. Whistle-blowers raised concerns involving 'some of the biggest companies, like Cognizant,' though the watchdog stressed it has not accused Cognizant of wrongdoing. Dozens of subpoenas have already been issued, tied to a White House fraud task force.
key_points: US Labor Dept watchdog opens major H-1B and green-card fraud probe
Alleges wage kickbacks, forced labour and displacement of US workers
Whistle-blowers named big firms 'like Cognizant' — no wrongdoing charged yet
Dozens of subpoenas issued; Indians hold the bulk of H-1B visas
cards.moot: Cross-border industries depend on the rules of the country they serve — a labour-mobility programme like the H-1B is a policy lever, and tightening it can reshape an entire foreign industry's cost base and strategy without a single tariff being raised.

depth.background: The H-1B is the main US work visa for skilled foreign professionals, and Indians receive the large majority of them each year — it is the backbone of the India-US technology pipeline and a key channel for Indian IT firms to staff American projects. The PERM system is the related process for employment-based green cards (permanent residency). The Trump administration has made cracking down on visa 'fraud' and protecting American jobs a priority.
depth.why_it_matters: This directly touches India's roughly $250bn IT-services industry and hundreds of thousands of Indian professionals. Tighter scrutiny — more audits, subpoenas and denials — raises costs and uncertainty for firms that rotate staff to the US, and the naming of a large outsourcer signals the probe reaches the sector's core. It also fits a broader US turn toward protecting domestic jobs, which could reshape how Indian IT delivers work to American clients.
depth.ripple_effects: Indian IT firms may lean harder on local US hiring and offshore/automated delivery to reduce visa exposure, and the overhang can weigh on sector sentiment just as TCS opens earnings season. For Indian professionals, it means more paperwork, longer waits and higher rejection risk. Diplomatically, it adds friction to an otherwise deepening India-US relationship.
depth.why_now: The Inspector General publicly launched the probe and issued subpoenas this week, framing it as the administration's first major H-1B/PERM fraud investigation.
depth.watch_next: Whether the probe names specific firms with formal charges, any policy tightening on H-1B rules, and how Indian IT majors address visa exposure on their earnings calls.
depth.market_link: A sentiment overhang for Indian IT (TCS, Infosys, Wipro, HCLTech) and US-listed outsourcers like Cognizant; raises the value of local US hiring and AI-led delivery.

key_terms.definition: A US work visa for skilled foreign professionals in specialty jobs; Indians receive the majority each year.
The US labour-certification process employers use to sponsor a foreign worker for an employment-based green card (permanent residency).
A legal order requiring a person or company to hand over documents or give testimony in an investigation.

cards.title: Le Pen launches her 2027 campaign — ankle monitor and all
cards.what_happened: A day after that ruling reopened her path, Le Pen declared she will run for the presidency in 2027, saying the court-ordered monitor will not stop her, and began campaigning to sharply mixed reactions across France — supporters energised, opponents warning that a convicted politician is back in the race. Her return keeps her, rather than 30-year-old protege Jordan Bardella, as the far right's likely standard-bearer.
key_points: Le Pen says she'll run for president in 2027 despite her conviction
A court upheld her fraud conviction but shortened the office ban
She must wear a court-ordered electronic monitor
Reaction in France is sharply divided
The far right is France's poll frontrunner heading into 2027
cards.moot: In modern democracies the exact terms of a sentence — which months are suspended, whether a ban is backdated — can decide whether a frontrunner is knocked out or set free, so 'political risk' now includes judicial calendars as much as ballot boxes.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported the appeals court upholding Le Pen's fraud conviction but shortening and backdating her office ban so it had effectively expired — clearing a path for her to run in 2027, with a jail term (mostly suspended) and a one-year ankle tag attached.
thread.changed: She has now acted on that opening — declaring she will run for president in 2027 despite the monitor and launching her campaign to sharply divided reactions in France.

depth.background: Marine Le Pen has led France's far right for over a decade and tops polls for the April 2027 presidential race. She was convicted of embezzling European Parliament funds — using EU money meant for parliamentary assistants to pay party staff — and a lower court had barred her from public office, which would have ended her candidacy. On appeal this week, a court upheld the conviction but shortened and backdated the office ban so it has effectively expired, while imposing a mostly-suspended jail term and a one-year electronic ankle monitor.
depth.why_it_matters: It restores the poll-leading frontrunner to Europe's most consequential coming election. Le Pen's candidacy sharpens a democratic tension seen across the West — courts enforcing the law against politicians versus voters' right to choose — and, because her fiscal programme is high-spending and Eurosceptic, it keeps a political-risk premium simmering in French government bonds and the euro.
depth.ripple_effects: French politics reorganises around a live Le Pen run; markets track it because a credible far-right path has previously unsettled French bonds (where the state borrows) and pressured the euro. Any further appeal to France's top court, or a tightening of the monitor's conditions, could still complicate her campaign.
depth.why_now: The appeals ruling on 7 July removed the bar to her candidacy, and she moved immediately — within a day — to declare and start campaigning.
depth.watch_next: Whether prosecutors or Le Pen appeal further, how the ankle-monitor conditions affect her ability to campaign, and early 2027 polling.
depth.market_link: French government bonds and the euro stay sensitive to a live far-right path to the 2027 presidency; Le Pen's campaign keeps that political-risk premium in play.

key_terms.definition: An ankle-worn tag that tracks a person's location as part of a criminal sentence, restricting where they can go.
A legal bar on holding or running for public office, sometimes imposed as part of a criminal sentence — here shortened enough to expire.

cards.title: Markets
cards.subtitle: War risk goes live: the US and Iran trade strikes, oil rockets toward $80 and Hormuz shipping halts — Indian stocks suffer their worst day in months, the rupee hits a record low, and TCS opens earnings season tonight into the storm.

cards.story: Yesterday's scare became today's shock. After Iran struck three tankers in the Strait of Hormuz — the sea gate for about a fifth of the world's oil — the US hit more than 80 targets across Iran and 60-plus Revolutionary Guard boats, Iran fired back at US bases in Bahrain and Kuwait, and Trump declared the ceasefire 'over.' Oil did what it always does when that waterway is threatened: US crude (WTI) leapt about 4.6% to ~$73.7 and Brent pushed toward $80, while tanker traffic through Hormuz 'essentially stopped.' The pain flows straight to India, which imports ~85% of its oil and routes roughly 40% of its crude, 60% of its gas and 90% of its cooking gas through that one strait — so the Nifty crashed 2.1% to 23,882, the Sensex sank 1,677 points to 76,504 (the worst session in over three months), banks led the fall (Bank Nifty -2.5%), and the rupee tumbled 59 paise to a record-low 95.55 per dollar. Two other forces made the day worse: the Federal Reserve's first minutes under new chair Kevin Warsh revealed a split committee more worried about inflation than rate cuts — a hawkish tilt that firmed the dollar and, counter-intuitively, dragged gold down 1.5% to ~$4,084 even on a war day (higher-for-longer rates raise the cost of holding metal that pays no interest). The one green shoot was in chips: a report that China will let its top AI firms buy limited quantities of Nvidia's H200 lifted Nvidia ~3% against an otherwise red US tape. Among your favorites, Apple ($314.03, +1.08%) actually rose to a fresh record-area high as money hid in mega-cap quality; Nvidia ($203.05, +3.11%) was the day's standout on the China news and got upgraded to Buy; Reliance (~₹1,275.9, ~-1.3%) slid to a fresh 52-week-low zone as the selloff hit and higher crude squeezes refining margins; and Bitcoin (~$62,096, -1.9%) sank toward one-year lows as risk-off, ETF outflows and a no-cut Fed kept the downtrend intact. All of this lands as TCS reports Q1 results tonight — the market's first earnings reality-check, arriving on its ugliest day in months.
themes: Energy / oil & gas: The US-Iran war sent WTI +4.6% and Brent toward $80 with Hormuz shipping halted — a big war premium that lifts oil producers but taxes import-heavy economies and fuel-consuming sectors.
Indian equities: The worst day in months — Nifty -2.1%, Sensex -1,677 pts, Bank Nifty -2.5%, rupee at a record low — as the oil shock hit the most oil-import-dependent large market first.
Gold & safe havens: Gold FELL 1.5% despite the war, as hawkish Fed minutes and a firm dollar raised the cost of holding non-yielding metal — the haven bid went to oil, not gold.
AI & semiconductors: The rare bright spot: China easing H200 purchases lifted Nvidia ~3% and helped the Nasdaq claw back to near-flat, bucking the broader risk-off tape.
cards.moot: War risk goes live: the US and Iran trade strikes, oil rockets toward $80 and Hormuz shipping halts — Indian stocks suffer their worst day in months, the rupee hits a record low, and TCS opens earnings season tonight into the storm.

global_summary.us: US stocks fell as the US-Iran escalation and rising oil weighed — the Dow dropped ~1% (~500 points) and the S&P slipped 0.36%, while the Nasdaq clawed back to near-flat (-0.08%), cushioned by a ~3% jump in Nvidia on news China will allow limited H200 chip purchases.
global_summary.india: The worst day in over three months: the Nifty crashed 2.1% to 23,882 and the Sensex sank 1,677 points to 76,504, with banks leading (Bank Nifty -2.5%) as the oil shock hit an import-dependent market; the rupee fell 59 paise to a record-low 95.55/$. TCS reports Q1 tonight.
global_summary.crypto: Bitcoin fell ~1.9% to ~$62,096 toward a one-year low and Ether -1.9%, dragged by the risk-off mood, persistent ETF outflows and a Fed that won't cut — crypto still trading as a risk asset, not a haven.
global_summary.macro: WTI crude jumped ~4.6% to ~$73.7 (Brent toward $80) as the US and Iran traded strikes and Hormuz traffic halted; gold fell 1.5% to ~$4,084 on a hawkish Fed and firm dollar, and the VIX rose ~5% to ~16.9.

catalysts_today: 9 July, after close (IN) — TCS Q1 FY27 results + interim dividend; call 7pm IST
Ongoing — Rupee at record low; RBI intervention watch
Overnight (US) — AI-chip trade after China H200 easing report
risks: Oil/war shock: an escalating US-Iran conflict or any move to close Hormuz would push crude higher, widen India's import bill, pressure the rupee and revive inflation — the biggest cross-book risk today.
Rupee weakness: at a record-low 95.55/$, further falls raise import and dollar-debt costs and can trigger foreign-investor selling and RBI intervention.
Earnings disappointment: a soft TCS print tonight could puncture the IT sector and deepen the selloff on an already-fragile day.
Hawkish Fed: minutes showed inflation worries and possible hikes — a firmer dollar keeps the rupee soft, pressures gold and rate-sensitive tech, and caps any relief rally.
Reversal risk in relief trades: Nvidia's bounce rests partly on a policy report that could reverse, and a deeper risk-off wave would drag even resilient mega-caps like Apple.

cards.moot: Wall Street's 'fear gauge' jumped ~5% as the war shock lifted hedging demand — still historically low, but rising; India's own volatility gauge spiked far more.

items.name: VIX (volatility)
items.read: Wall Street's 'fear gauge' jumped ~5% as the war shock lifted hedging demand — still historically low, but rising; India's own volatility gauge spiked far more.

items.name: Crude Oil (WTI)
items.read: The day's big mover, +4.6% (Brent pushed toward $80) as the US and Iran traded strikes and Hormuz traffic halted — a full 'war premium' is back in energy.

items.name: Bank Nifty
items.read: Banks led the rout (-2.5%) — a higher oil price and record-low rupee revive inflation worries and trim the odds of the RBI rate cuts that lift lenders.

items.name: Sensex
items.read: The 30-stock index tanked 1,677 points, its worst day in over three months, as the oil shock hit an import-dependent market hardest — intraday it was down as much as 1,921 points.

items.name: Nifty 50
items.read: India's benchmark crashed 2.1% and lost the 24,000 mark — the cheap-oil tailwind that drove its rally flipped into a headwind as crude spiked on the US-Iran war.

items.name: Ethereum
items.read: Fell in step with Bitcoin (-1.9%) — the higher-beta crypto stays in a clear downtrend below its key moving averages on the same risk-off logic.

cards.title: US and Iran trade strikes — oil spikes and Hormuz shipping halts
cards.gist: The fragile US-Iran truce broke: after Iran struck three tankers in the Strait of Hormuz, the US hit more than 80 targets across Iran and 60-plus Revolutionary Guard boats, Iran fired back at US bases in Bahrain and Kuwait, and Trump declared the ceasefire 'over.' US crude jumped ~4.6% to ~$73.7 and Brent pushed toward $80 as tanker traffic through the strait stopped.
cards.what_it_means: Until this de-escalates, every Hormuz headline moves Indian stocks, the rupee and inflation expectations; a sustained spike is a real hit to India's outlook.
key_points: US hit 80+ Iran targets and 60+ Guard boats; Iran hit US bases in the Gulf
Trump: ceasefire 'over,' may hit Iran again; revoked Iran's oil licence
WTI +4.6% to ~$73.7; Brent toward $80; tanker traffic 'essentially stopped'
India, importing ~85% of its oil, is the biggest single loser
cards.moot: For an oil-importing country, a single event at a chokepoint can move the whole market — geography and geopolitics, not just how much is pumped, set the energy price and ripple into inflation, currency and stocks.

depth.why_it_matters: Oil is the single biggest lever on India's whole economy — it feeds petrol prices, inflation, the subsidy bill and the rupee. A live war at the chokepoint that carries roughly 40% of India's crude (and most of its gas) is the exact opposite of the cheap-oil tailwind that had powered the market — which is why the Nifty crashed and the rupee hit a record low today.
depth.watch: Whether the US strikes again as Trump threatened, whether Iran tries to close or mine Hormuz, and whether WTI holds above ~$73 or Brent breaks $80.

key_terms.definition: A narrow sea lane between Iran and Oman carrying roughly a fifth of the world's seaborne oil and much of its gas; there is no easy way around it.
The extra amount buyers pay for oil to insure against conflict cutting off supply, even before any barrel is actually lost.

cards.title: India's worst day in months — Sensex -1,677, rupee at a record low
cards.gist: Indian benchmarks suffered their sharpest selloff in over three months: the Sensex crashed 1,677 points (2.15%) to 76,504 and the Nifty fell 517 points (2.12%) to 23,882, with banks (Bank Nifty -2.5%) leading. The rupee tumbled 59 paise to a record-low 95.55 per dollar as costlier oil and a firmer dollar hit at once.
cards.what_it_means: Expect volatility and possible foreign-investor selling until oil settles; a weaker rupee also raises the cost of imports and dollar debt for Indian companies.
key_points: Bank Nifty -2.51% to 56,743 led the fall
Rupee sank 59 paise to a record-low 95.55/$
India VIX (volatility) spiked sharply as fear jumped
cards.moot: The force that drives a rally usually drives the reversal too — an oil-importing market that rose on cheap crude is the same market that falls fastest when a Gulf war sends the price back up.

depth.why_it_matters: This is how tightly India is chained to the oil price. Higher crude widens the import bill (India buys most of its oil in dollars), which weakens the rupee, feeds inflation and gives the RBI less room to cut rates — a chain that hits banks, real estate and consumer stocks. The size of the drop shows how much of the recent rally rested on cheap oil alone.
depth.watch: Whether the Nifty holds 23,800-24,000, foreign-investor (FII) flows, the rupee versus 96, and TCS's after-close results tonight.

key_terms.definition: The index of India's biggest banks; it is rate-sensitive, so it falls when higher oil/inflation reduce the chance of RBI rate cuts.
Money that foreign institutional investors move into or out of Indian stocks; heavy outflows can pull the market down in a risk-off phase.

cards.title: TCS opens earnings season tonight — the first reality-check
cards.gist: Tata Consultancy Services (TCS), India's largest IT-services firm, reports June-quarter results after the close today, with a 7pm IST call. Analysts expect revenue near Rs 72,012 crore (up ~1.9% quarter-on-quarter), roughly flat dollar revenue (~$7.6bn), and profit slipping ~2.5% as annual wage hikes weigh on margins — plus a likely interim dividend.
cards.what_it_means: Strong deal wins and upbeat AI commentary would lift Infosys, HCLTech and Wipro and support sentiment; a miss would hit IT hardest just as the Iran shock bites.
key_points: Q1 FY27 results after close today; earnings call at 7pm IST
Revenue seen ~Rs 72,012 cr (+1.9% QoQ); dollar revenue ~$7.6bn, ~flat
Profit seen dipping ~2.5% QoQ as wage hikes squeeze margins
Interim dividend expected; record date 15 July
cards.moot: The first big earnings report of a season carries outsized weight — it resets expectations for every peer that reports after it, so one company's numbers can move an entire sector.

depth.why_it_matters: TCS opens the season and sets the tone for the whole IT sector, which has been under a cloud over whether AI will disrupt it or drive new demand. Landing on the market's worst day in months, a reassuring print could steady nerves while a soft one would deepen the gloom — and its AI commentary matters most of all.
depth.watch: Revenue growth, the operating-margin number, large-deal wins, banking (BFSI) demand, the dividend, and AI commentary — on tonight's 7pm call.

key_terms.definition: The share of revenue left as profit after running costs — a key gauge of how efficiently a company turns sales into earnings.
India's first fiscal quarter — April to June 2026; Indian firms report on an April-March financial year.

cards.title: Hawkish Fed minutes firm the dollar — and gold falls even on a war day
cards.gist: The Fed's first minutes under new chair Kevin Warsh showed a committee that held rates unanimously but split on what's next — with many members expecting rates to end 2026 above today's level and inflation worries 'mounting.' That hawkish tilt firmed the dollar and helped push gold down 1.5% to ~$4,084 even as war fears raged.
cards.what_it_means: Don't assume gold rises in every crisis — watch the dollar and real yields; a hawkish Fed can override the fear bid, and it keeps pressure on rate-sensitive assets everywhere.
key_points: June minutes: unanimous 12-0 hold at 3.5%-3.75%
Split committee; many saw rates ending 2026 higher
Gold fell 1.5% to ~$4,084 as the dollar firmed
Next signal: the 29 July Fed rate decision
cards.moot: A safe haven is only 'safe' in the right conditions — gold competes with cash and bonds, so when rates and the dollar rise, even a war can fail to lift it. Watch what an asset does, not just what it's called.

depth.why_it_matters: This is a live lesson that gold isn't an automatic 'war hedge.' Higher-for-longer rates raise the opportunity cost of holding metal that pays no interest, and a stronger dollar makes dollar-priced gold pricier abroad — so on a day the oil-driven haven bid went into crude, gold actually fell. A firm dollar also keeps the rupee soft and foreign flows cautious toward India.
depth.watch: The 29 July Fed rate decision, the dollar index, and whether the oil spike pushes the next US inflation print high enough to revive hike talk.

key_terms.definition: What you give up by choosing one option over another — here, the interest you forgo by holding non-yielding gold instead of a bond.
A stance that favours higher interest rates to fight inflation, even at the cost of slower growth — the opposite of 'dovish.'

cards.title: The one bright spot: China cracks the door to Nvidia's H200
cards.gist: A report that China will let its top AI firms buy a limited amount of Nvidia's H200 chips lifted Nvidia about 3% to ~$203 — one of the few gainers on a day the broad US market fell. It marks a thaw in China's earlier reluctance to buy the coveted US hardware, and would help ease a global AI-chip shortage.
cards.what_it_means: A friendlier China channel supports Nvidia and AI-infrastructure names, but the approvals are 'limited' and case-by-case, so treat it as a crack in the door, not a re-opening.
key_points: China to let Alibaba, ByteDance, DeepSeek buy limited H200 chips
A softening of strict controls on advanced US AI chips
Nvidia jumped ~3% to ~$203, bucking the red tape
Helped the Nasdaq claw back to near-flat
cards.moot: In a market led by one theme, news that widens that theme's addressable market can lift a stock even against a falling tape — but policy-driven gains are only as durable as the policy behind them.

depth.why_it_matters: The AI-chip trade has been the market's load-bearing wall, so anything that widens Nvidia's biggest potential market is a genuine positive for the whole AI complex — and a rare offset to the war-driven risk-off mood. It also read through to Indian IT sentiment, which rides the same AI-demand story into TCS's results.
depth.watch: Whether the approvals are formalised and how large the quotas are, plus any US pushback on easier H200 sales to China.

key_terms.definition: Government limits on selling sensitive technology (like advanced chips) to certain countries, usually on national-security grounds.
A high-end Nvidia AI chip — powerful but a step below its very top models — sought after for training AI systems.

cards.name: NVIDIA Corp.
cards.whats_new: The day's standout — Nvidia jumped +3.11% to $203.05 (from $196.93) after a report that China will let its top AI firms buy limited H200 chips, and its technical rating was UPGRADED from Sell to Buy as RSI rose from ~44 to ~50. It's climbing back toward its 50-day line and out of laggard status.
cards.moot: Constructive

news.headline: China to let top AI firms buy limited Nvidia H200 chips, report says

depth.fundamentals: Mega-cap (~$5.0T) in Semiconductors, trailing P/E ~30 with a much lower forward P/E of ~15 — the market expects earnings to keep surging (revenue growing ~85% a year, keeping ~63 cents of every sales dollar as profit). Beta ~2.2 makes it far more volatile than the market; dividend yield ~0.5%.
depth.technicals: Transitional / sideways, now rated Buy: price ($203) is between its 200-day (~$191) and 50-day (~$210) averages, so reclaiming the ~$210 50-day line would be the next bullish signal. RSI ~50 is neutral. The China news is a fundamental catalyst; watch it hold above the ~$191 200-day support that defines its longer trend.
depth.catalysts: Formalisation of China's H200 approvals, the direction of the US AI/chip trade, and its own next earnings — any AI-demand headline moves it fast given its high beta.
depth.risks: High beta means outsized swings in a risk-off tape; the gain rests partly on a policy report that could reverse. A decisive break below the ~$191 200-day line would be bearish.

cards.name: Bitcoin
cards.whats_new: Fell another -1.9% to ~$62,096, sliding toward fresh one-year lows as the war-driven risk-off tape, steady ETF outflows and a no-cut Fed all weighed. Its technical rating slipped to Sell (from Neutral), confirming the downtrend rather than a dip to buy.
cards.moot: Cautious

news.headline: Bitcoin under pressure as US-Iran escalation lifts oil

depth.fundamentals: The largest cryptocurrency by value (~$1.24T market cap). Bitcoin pays no yield, so its appeal falls when 'safe' bond returns are high — and a Fed that has ruled out 2026 cuts keeps those yields attractive versus non-yielding crypto.
depth.technicals: Downtrend, rated Sell: price is below both its 50-day (~$65,600) and 200-day (~$74,000) averages, near the bottom of its 52-week range, with RSI ~46. It lost the ~$62k support area; a decisive break risks a fresh leg lower, while reclaiming ~$63k would be an early stabilisation sign.
depth.catalysts: US spot-ETF flow data, the 29 July Fed decision, and the risk-on/off mood in AI/tech that crypto now tracks closely.
depth.risks: Near a one-year low with the downtrend intact; persistent institutional ETF outflows and a no-cut Fed are the main overhangs, and a risk-off market keeps pressure on speculative assets.

cards.name: Reliance Industries
cards.whats_new: Slid ~1.3% to about Rs 1,275.9 (day range ~Rs 1,271.6-1,302.3) in the broad selloff, dropping to a fresh 52-week-low zone. The oil spike is a mixed blessing for a refiner — higher crude can squeeze refining margins even as it's an energy name. (Note: today's automated feed returned no fresh price for Indian tickers, so this uses the cited 8 July market close.)
cards.moot: Cautious

news.headline: Sensex crashes 1,677 pts, Nifty ends at 23,882 on US-Iran tension

depth.fundamentals: Mega-cap (~Rs 17T) in Energy at a moderate trailing P/E of ~22 (forward P/E ~18 implies expected growth). Revenue is growing ~12% a year and it keeps ~8 cents of every sales dollar as profit; beta ~0.18 makes it much steadier than the market. Dividend yield ~0.5%.
depth.technicals: Downtrend near 52-week lows: the stock has been sliding below its 50-day (~Rs 1,335) and 200-day (~Rs 1,415) averages, and RSI ~48 is neutral. Support to hold is the recent ~Rs 1,272 low; a close back above ~Rs 1,335 would be the first sign of a turn. The broad-market crash and oil-margin worries are the near-term overhang.
depth.catalysts: Crude and refining-margin trends, any news on retail/Jio/new-energy verticals, and broad India market direction through earnings season.
depth.risks: Trading at fresh 52-week lows in a confirmed downtrend; a break below ~Rs 1,272 support opens more downside. Higher crude can pressure refining economics, and a weak overall market weighs on the heaviest index stock.

cards.context_title: Tankers struck in the Strait of Hormuz — the US–Iran truce cracks and oil jumps
cards.question: Yesterday we decoded this story. What's the transferable lesson?
cards.answer: A chokepoint is leverage: when a fifth of the world's oil must squeeze through one narrow strait, a single attack there can move global prices more than a big change in how much is pumped — geography, not just supply, sets the energy price.

also_notable.title: IOC lifts Russia's suspension, clearing Russian athletes to return for the 2028 Olympics
also_notable.why: Ends a decade-long ban rooted in a doping scandal and the Ukraine invasion — a big shift in how sport treats Russia even as the war grinds on.

also_notable.title: US charges an Indian crime figure over the assassination of a Sikh activist
also_notable.why: Reopens a case that strained India-Canada ties; a sensitive test of how allegations of transnational violence are handled, presented as claims not yet proven.

also_notable.title: A German palliative-care doctor is jailed for life for murdering 15 patients
also_notable.why: One of Germany's gravest medical-murder cases, with investigators suspecting the true toll is higher — a rare, chilling breach of medical trust.

also_notable.title: A homegrown catnip lotion repels mosquitoes as well as DEET, a Uganda trial finds
also_notable.why: A cheap, locally-grown alternative to the standard chemical could help fight malaria and dengue where imported repellents are costly.

cards.disclaimer: Decoded from public reporting for general understanding. Verify before relying on any single claim; details evolve. Not financial advice.
